speaker: interviewer
text: --for the American Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: I made my way in with the American Army. And they-- I worked my way up. And I worked for the 97th Signal Company till-- for almost a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in-- I was charge in the mess hall. I was given the duty-- first of all, the stockroom. They keep track of the-- of the rations to allocate the food for the daily need for the kitchen. And to-- and then, later on, to write up the requisitions that were needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed with them for about a year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, me. For about eight months, I stayed with them, because they eventually evacuated. The Camp Wels was evacuated. And they were taken into Linz, L-I-N-Z, Austria. And they were giving some more substantial lodging. The, the American government, military government expropriated a huge-- a huge block-- or blocks, I should say, of apartments that they allocated to the former concentration camp inmates. And there they were-- they were governed under the UNRRA at that time-- U-N-R-A-- and also the Joint-- the American Joint Distribution Committee. Later on, the UNRRA was transferred over to the IRO, to the International Refugee Organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in the Linz village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I stayed in Wels. But the camp was evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now I got to be very well known in the-- in the camp because, every night, I took-- you know the Americans, how generous they are with, with dishing out food and how much waste there is when we weren't used to it. So me being in charge, more or less, in the mess hall, I told my mess sergeant-- who incidentally was Jewish. I told him, look, there is a camp here, you know, with some poor people from the concentration camp. People-- why throw out all this food? Anything that's left over, let's take it over to-- to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I was allowed to wear American uniform, military uniform. So I drove-- we drove into the camp every night. We took in the leftovers, whatever there was. And we gave it to them. And so they knew me as the American that's coming. The American is-- this is how I got to know my wife, too. But when they evacuated the camp from Wels, I lost track with them. But it was in January of 1946 that I had nothing to do in Wels. I was the only Jew there. And maybe there was the two tailors and a barber that was-- stayed on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went into Linz to see my old buddies there. And there I visited my wife. And one thing led to another. And before too long, we got married. On January 27, we got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I gave notice to the Signal Company. I told them that I'm-- I stayed on for a while, but not for too long. And I moved into Linz. In Linz, I went into the 14th Regulating Station of the Transportation Corps. And I presented myself as a knowledgeable person running a mess hall. And I was immediately hired. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was another division of the US Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was another division of the USA forces. We were in charge. Our, our-- we were in charge of anything that was moving. Anything mechanical moving on the surface, or in the air had to get our permission. It came from our headquarters. We were the headquarters there. And I worked there. I was given the duty of supervising the five mess halls and to do the requisitions for them, to make up the menus for them on daily basis. And this had to be done per capita. You know, you-- but we ran a very, very model operation, so much so that we were-- we were commended. And we were visited by many officers from different regions, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there till 1948, till June or July 1948, at which time I gave it up because the inspections became too frequent. And it was very strenuous. You had to be on your toes all the time. And you know, you couldn't have your-- your rations had to balance to the T all the time. And you had to do something about it, you know, to hide things. And, you know. So it became very strenuous. So I gave it up. And I worked for the AJDC for about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] five months, I would say-- five or six months. I was their representative in Camp Steyr. I was the social worker at--

speaker: interviewer
text: For the Joint Distribution?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the Joint representative in Camp Steyr. I was traveling from Linz to Steyr on daily basis. My office was, was in Steyr. And the Joint's duty-- your-- you know-- we were-- we were given the task to look after all the Romanian refugees that came across. They had to come across from [INAUDIBLE], from the Russian-dominated side, sector, into the American sector. And we were given the task to look after those people. When they come through there, they should be fed. And they should be given, if necessary, some clothing, whatever they needed. Put them onto a truck, and to ship them further. They went either to Salzburg, or-- and from Salzburg, they went to Italy or to Munich. And they, they were dispersed. At that time, I made some great efforts to get into-- to the United States. But just to backtrack a little bit, I still didn't know anything that I had a sur-- another surviving sister. I know-- I knew that I had two surviving sisters, one in Israel and the one that was in hiding. But the third one, I didn't know what happened to her. But in June of 1945-- or July. I believe it was July, because in July, I found out that she was somewhere in a hospital in Germany because she was very sick. And then in August, I under-- I got word that she came back to my hometown, to Satu Mare. And that she eventually married my pre-war brother-in-law, who lost his wife and six children in, in Auschwitz. She never came back. And so he married her. Eventually, they wanted to leave Romania as well. So they came to Vienna. And from Vienna-- by that time, I was very well connected with the Brihah. I had them come over to the American side. I arranged for all their papers. Eventually-- they stayed with-- with me for about almost a year. And they decided that they want to do something for themselves. And they made tracks first to Paris, France. I made sure that my sister was taken across the German border. They went to France. They stayed in Paris several months. And from Paris, they went to Israel. In Israel, they-- it was a very bad time in 1948. I don't have to tell you. He is a master craftsman in bookbinding. And he eventually-- he opened up a-- a fair-size bookbinding factory. And they re-established the family. They have two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: As for myself, I got married and-- on January 27th. And I went through the-- the regular life. But I had the requisition put in for admission to the United States. And I had some great pull. My commanding officer kind of took me personally down to the American consulate. And I have some terrific credentials that I, I can show. But the American Immigration was very slow in, in granting a, a visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the meantime, a commission came out from Canada that they were recruiting furriers. And I got word. I got word that I should register all those furriers in Camp Steyr that desired to immigrate to Canada, which I did. And I registered 14 of 'em.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the day came when the commission was to sit in Linz to examine these people. I was to take them in and to present them to the commission. And by that time, my superior was a Scottish lady who was working for the IRO. She was sitting at the table with one member of the commission.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as I was presenting the furriers, the applicants, one by one, I sort of bent over to her-- and I forgot her name-- Miss So-And-So. I says, what do you think? Should I put in an application for-- to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, sure, go. It's a very nice country. But I'm not a furrier.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the man from the commission was sitting there, who was a Jewish fellow. And I said-- she bent to him. He says, this man is a very good man. He worked for us, you know. And you know, he would like to go to Canada. So he turns to me. He says, well, are you a furrier? I says, uh, no. He said, do you have any relatives in Canada? I says, I don't. But my wife has. Who are they? Ladowsky, they have a restaurant. As it happens, they have a very famous restaurant in Toronto, whom he knew very well. He says, Ladowsky is the relative? He says, OK. He says, you have to go through an examination. You think you can do it? I says, I'll try. I know something about what furriers do. So when I came to the officials from the IRO and they said, are you a furrier? I said, yes. What can you do? I'm a blocker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now a blocker-- they didn't have-- they had facilities. They had sewing machines. And they had hand sewing. But they didn't-- didn't have facilities to-- to test blockers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I'm a blocker. Oh, you're a blocker? Good. Stamped. Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through all the rigmarole with all the examination with the Canadian officials, with, you know, political, and literacy, and health, you name it. And we finally got the visa to go to Canada. So in October-- on October 1948, we arrived into Canada after a horrendous voyage that we had. My wife was, at that time, five months pregnant. And my first son was born in Canada in February of 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go straight to Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Straight to Toronto. And I lived there ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you received in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were received exceedingly well. Because we went into-- our first station was to go into the restaurant to have breakfast. The whole gang in my group was to come into that restaurant-- that was sponsored also by the Federation-- to have breakfast in her relatives' restaurant. We were very well received. And they took us into their house. And we stayed with them for some length of time till we acquired our own place. And I got a job as a shipper in a ladies coat and suit factory that also belonged to a relative of my wife's. And after a short while, having had the experience in the textile industry, a textile sales-- salesman, I made myself rather useful. And before too long, I got, little by little, promoted. And we had a second son. And in 1956, I left my position. And I established my own business. And I had a factory with partners. We employed, at one time, close to 100 people. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in 1986, after 30 years being in business, I retired. And we live in Toronto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We really appreciate your doing this interview for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- in your-- how come you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, two reasons. First of all, I was rather young. I was 20 years old. There are certain things, like for instance, when we arrived in Bernau, there was no more train. We had to go through the mountains. And we-- I slept-- overnight, I slept on the ground. It was freezing cold. And I survived, you know. And in Bergen-Belsen, I lost my shoes. And I walked in the snow. And I survived. I didn't catch a cold. I was young. And we hope-- although death or-- was hovering over us and, we-- more or less, in the back of our mind, we didn't think that we will survive. But we-- on the other part of the brain told us that we will survive, and we, we, we will come out of it. And-- And then it probably-- we didn't quite comprehend the, the gravity of the, of the situation that we were in. We knew that it's bad. We knew-- we saw all the corpses that were taken away. We saw they were-- they were dropping out. But somehow, we, we had the feeling that we will make it. As a matter of fact, I-- I'll never forget the one situation when the Swedish Red Cross made an inspection in Bergen-Belsen at our camp. And the rumor was going around that there is a commission from Sweden here. And I met one of the guys that was coming from the washroom. And he used the expression-- he says, the commission is here. It looks like maybe after all, we are not going to perish. That was his remark, which was quite an expression. But that was the general feeling, that we were ready to perish. But nonetheless, we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you always had hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we, we did. At one, one place-- at one time, we thought that we are for sure condemned. On the other hand, there was that spark, spark of hope that, that we nurtured.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any particular message you would like for your grandchildren to know from all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like them to know of what we went through. [SOBBING] And that it should never happen again. [CRYING] [DRINKS] It was-- it was a period of history that is really written in blood. And they should know that, despite everything, we maintained our heritage and that we should conserve it and keep it forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Linz, Austria. And it was taken in my-- at the end of 1947. I was employed that time at the American Occupation Forces. And we had a rather good life by that time. And we bounced back. And we lived a very acceptable and comfortable life, as you can see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my wife [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that is hanging onto my right. And that's me. And I was-- at that time, I was going into 24-- 24 years old. And we were reasonably satisfied by that time, except for the memories that we had to carry along with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
